1.synthesis: The farewell discourse closes in prayer. Jesus lifts his eyes toward heaven: "Father, the hour has come; glorify your Son that the Son may glorify you" (v.1). He has been given authority over all flesh, to give eternal life to all whom the Father has given him — and eternal life is this: "to know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent" (v.3). He has glorified the Father on earth, having accomplished the work he was given to do (v.4). Now he asks: "glorify me in your own presence with the glory that I had with you before the world existed" (v.5).
1.historical_context: The prayer called "High Priestly" (a name coined by Chytraeus in 1561) draws on the priestly consecration vocabulary of Leviticus: the verb hagiazō (sanctify/consecrate) in vv.17–19 is the LXX term for the consecration of priests. The upward look toward heaven was the standard Jewish prayer posture. "The only true God" (v.3) asserts Shema monotheism against Roman polytheism. The phrase "eternal life is this: knowing" (v.3) invokes the covenantal da'at YHWH (knowledge of God) of Jeremiah 31:34 and Hosea 6:6 — relational knowing, not propositional. The petition to restore pre-incarnate glory (v.5) echoes the Prologue's "in the beginning" (John 1:1) and the Wisdom tradition's description of Wisdom present before creation (Proverbs 8:22–31). The verb "finished" (eteleiósa, v.4) anticipates "It is finished" (tetelestai) from the cross (John 19:30) — same root, framing the entire ministry as a priestly service brought to completion through death.
1.literary_structure: The prayer is structured in three concentric circles: the Son (vv.1–5), the immediate disciples (vv.6–19), and the future community of believers (vv.20–26). This first section establishes the prayer's Christological ground: the mutual glorification of Father and Son through the completed mission. The arrival of the hour (v.1) — deferred throughout the Gospel (John 2:4; John 7:30; John 12:23) — is now simply stated. The definition of eternal life in v.3 is the Gospel's most compact doctrinal summary: life is relationship with God through the one God sent. The petition for pre-incarnate glory (v.5) frames the passion as a temporary kenosis about to be reversed — a descent and return that the Prologue already narrated as the Word's movement.
1.theological_application: "Eternal life is this: that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent" (v.3) is the Gospel's most precise definition of what is being offered throughout. Eternal life is not primarily a temporal category (living forever) but a relational one (knowing the living God). This shifts the horizon of faith from destination to relationship: the life that is already beginning in faith is the same life that continues eternally, because it consists in knowing the inexhaustible God and his Son. The completion of the work (v.4) spoken in the present tense — "I have glorified you; I have accomplished the work" — treats the cross as already complete in the prayer's perspective, reflecting the Gospel's consistent view that Jesus approaches the passion not as a victim but as a priest who knows the purpose and end of his offering.
1.christology: "Glorify me in your own presence with the glory that I had with you before the world existed" (v.5) is the most explicit pre-existence claim in the Gospel's narrative, exceeding even "before Abraham was, I am" (John 8:58). The glory existed before the world — before creation — in the shared life of Father and Son. The Incarnation was the setting aside of that glory for the mission; the passion is the path back to it. This grounds the cross not in necessity imposed from outside but in the Son's willing participation in a movement he chose: he came from glory, took on flesh, accomplished the work, and now asks to return. The prayer presents Calvary as the final act of an obedience that began before time.

6.synthesis: Jesus prays for those the Father has given him from the world. He has revealed the Father's name to them; they have received the words given to Jesus and believed that the Father sent him (vv.6–8). He does not pray for the world but for these — "Holy Father, keep them in your name, which you have given me, that they may be one, even as we are one" (v.11). He kept them while he was with them; none was lost except the son of destruction, that Scripture would be fulfilled (v.12). He does not ask for their removal from the world but for their protection from the evil one (v.15). "Sanctify them in the truth; your word is truth. As you sent me into the world, so I have sent them into the world. And for their sake I consecrate myself, that they also may be sanctified in truth" (vv.17–19).
6.historical_context: "I have revealed your name to them" (v.6) invokes the whole force of the divine Name (ha-Shem) in Jewish tradition — not a label but the full character of God, disclosed covenantally and never pronounced. The disciples given by the Father are described in election language that echoes throughout chapters 6 and 17: they were the Father's before they were given to the Son (v.6). The "son of destruction" (v.12) uses the Semitic construction of a person defined by their destiny (cf. 2 Thessalonians 2:3); the Judas reference is confirmed by the Scripture-fulfillment note. The sanctification petition (vv.17–19) uses hagiazō — the priestly consecration term of Leviticus 8 — making the disciples' sending into the world an extension of the priestly mission. The distinction between not praying "for the world" (v.9) and keeping the disciples in the world (v.15) is deliberate: the scope of intercession follows the covenant community structure (the Day of Atonement precedent), while the mission orientation remains universal.
6.literary_structure: The prayer for the disciples unfolds in three moves: their identity and reception of the revelation (vv.6–8), the petition for their preservation in the Father's name and unity (vv.9–12), and the petition for their sanctification and mission (vv.13–19). The structural center is the tension between the world and the disciples: they are in the world but not of it (vv.14–16), which requires both protection and sending. "For their sake I consecrate myself" (v.19) is the priestly act that grounds the disciples' consecration: the high priest's self-sanctification enables the community's sanctification. The prayer presses the Johannine theology of representation: the community's standing before God is grounded in the Son's standing, not in their own achieved holiness.
6.theological_application: The prayer for the disciples' unity in the Father's name (v.11) grounds the community's unity in the most stable foundation imaginable: the unity of Father and Son. Human communities typically achieve unity through shared ethnicity, ideology, geography, or interest — all of which fragment under sufficient pressure. The Johannine community's unity is sourced in a reality that cannot fragment. The protection request of v.15 — not removal from the world but keeping from the evil one — establishes the community's missionary posture as the design, not withdrawal. Sanctification through truth (v.17) grounds the community's moral formation in the revelation of the Father, not in ritual or social boundary-marking: truth, received and inhabited, is the instrument of consecration.
6.christology: "For their sake I consecrate myself, that they also may be sanctified in truth" (v.19) is the prayer's most concentrated atonement-language. The Son's self-consecration for the disciples' sanctification is the priestly act that makes their mission possible: they are sent into the world as sanctified ones because the one who sends them has consecrated himself as the offering. The cross, approached in prayer before it is reached in narrative, is here framed not as defeat imposed from outside but as priestly self-offering — the final act of a high priesthood exercised throughout the ministry and completed at the altar of Golgotha. The community's sanctification is the fruit of the Son's passion.

20.synthesis: The prayer's vision expands: Jesus prays not only for the disciples present but for all who will believe through their word (v.20). The petition is unity: "that they may all be one, just as you, Father, are in me, and I in you, that they also may be in us, so that the world may believe that you have sent me" (v.21). He has given them the glory the Father gave him — so that they may be one as Father and Son are one, in each other — that the world may know that the Father has sent the Son and loved the disciples as he loved the Son (vv.22–23). He desires that they be with him where he is, to see his glory, given to him in love before the foundation of the world (v.24). He has made the Father's name known and will continue to make it known, "that the love with which you have loved me may be in them, and I in them" (v.26).
20.historical_context: The extension of the prayer to "those who believe through their word" (v.20) establishes the apostolic chain of testimony as the means of the community's future growth — future believers come to faith through the eyewitness word, making the apostolic testimony structurally irreplaceable. The unity petitioned — modeled on Trinitarian unity (vv.21–22) — deploys the perfect-tense perfection language (teleioō, v.23) that links the community's unity with Jesus's own completed work (v.4) and the cross's finality (John 19:30). The petition to "see my glory" (v.24) echoes Moses's request in Exodus 33:18; what Moses was not granted in full, the disciples are promised. The claim that the Father loved the Son "before the foundation of the world" grounds the entire redemptive economy in eternal, pre-creational love — love that is the ground, not the consequence, of the mission.
20.literary_structure: The prayer reaches its widest scope here, moving from the Son (vv.1–5) to the immediate disciples (vv.6–19) to the entire future community (vv.20–26). The unity petitions of vv.21–23 form the theological core: unity is simultaneously modeled on the Trinitarian relationship, sustained by participation in it, and directed outward toward the world's belief. The prayer closes not with a petition but with a statement of the ongoing mission: "I have made your name known... and I will continue to make it known" (v.26). The making-known that the ministry has achieved is not complete at the cross; it continues through the Spirit, through the apostolic testimony, and through the community's unity. The final word — "I in them" — closes the prayer with the mutual indwelling that has been the Gospel's central promise since chapter 14.
20.theological_application: The petition that the community's unity might lead to the world's belief (v.21) makes the visible unity of the church an instrument of mission. This is not an optional or secondary concern: the purpose clause — "so that the world may believe" — makes unity essential to the community's witness. A community fractured by pride, competition, and division does not merely fail aesthetically; it actively contradicts the message it proclaims. Conversely, a community in which the love of God is visibly present — in which people are drawn together by the same love with which the Father loves the Son — is the most powerful testimony available. The prayer does not specify what institutional or organizational form this unity must take, but it insists on its necessity and grounds it in the only source capable of producing it: participation in the divine life.
20.christology: "The glory that you have given me I have given to them" (v.22) is the most audacious claim of the prayer. The glory that was the eternal property of the Son — the pre-incarnate glory of v.5 — is being transferred to the community. This is not the conferral of a status symbol but a participation in the relational reality that constitutes the divine life: the community is drawn into the love between Father and Son and begins to live from within that love. The love the Father has for the Son is the same love he has for the community (v.23, v.26): identical in character and quality, extended outward through the Son to those he has called. The prayer closes on this note of radical inclusion: the community is not a spectator of divine love but its destination and dwelling.
